source_texts: “I mean those clubs, those Dussot’s, and those spits of land,§ perhaps, that’s progress for you—well, that’ll all be without me,” he continued, once more taking no note of the question. “Besides, who wants to be a cardsharp?”
‘When I wished to determine the increase in weight the Nautilus would require to dive, my only concern was the progressive reduction in volume that sea water experiences as it becomes deeper.’
“That is not right, it’s a mistake!” Nina Alexandrovna intervened suddenly, looking almost ruefully at the Prince. “Mon mari se trompe.” *
“Sit down, Ivan, please, here on the sofa,” Alyosha said, frightened. “You’re feverish. Lie down. Put your head on the pillow. Right. Would you like a wet towel on your head? It might help.”

He told them. But we shall not repeat it here. He told the story briefly and impersonally. He did not speak of his love and his feelings; he told them only that he was no longer so determined to shoot himself “in view of certain new developments.” He did not bother to explain the motives behind his actions anymore, did not go into subtleties. Besides, his interrogators did not interrupt him very often to ask him questions; it was obvious that this was not what interested them most.
“Wait, you didn’t get that from me!” Ivan suddenly stopped, struck by this fact. “It never even occurred to me . . . Strange!”
yells Mikolka, the crowbar still in his hands, his eyes bloodshot. He stands there as if feeling sorry that there’s nothing more to strike.

As for the man, he truly was so tired that he did not even make the most of those clean white sheets. He snuffed out his candle with his nostril, the way convicts do, and dropping on to the bed fully dressed, immediately fell into a deep sleep.

The truth is that no one has ever really explained how Joseph Buquet met his death. The verdict at the inquest was ‘suicide under natural circumstances’. In his Memoirs of a Theatre Director, Armand Moncharmin, one of the co-directors who succeeded Messrs Debienne and Poligny, relates the incident as follows: A most unfortunate accident ruined the little party that Messrs Debienne and Poligny were giving to mark their departure.

A thousand lords with girdle-jade walk in front and rear. The many flags of guardsmen stand both left and right. Those holding gilt bludgeons, And halberds and axes, March in pairs and pairs; The red silk lanterns, The royal incense urn, Move in solemnity. The dragons fly and the phoenixes dance; The falcons soar and the eagles take wing. This Son of Heaven’s an upright sage; The righteous ministers are good. They increase our bliss by a thousand years, surpassing Yu and Shun; They secure peace of ten thousand ages, rivaling Yao and Tang. We also see the curve-handled umbrella, And robes with rolling dragons— Their glare lighting up each other; The jade joined-rings, The phoenix fans, Waving through holy mist. Those caps of pearls and belts of jade; The purple sashes and medals of gold. A thousand rows of soldiers protect the Throne; Two lines of marshals uphold the carriage. This emperor, cleansed and sincere, bows to the Buddha, Glad to raise incense and seek virtue’s fruit. The grand cortege of the Tang emperor soon arrived in front of the temple. The emperor ordered a halt to the music, left the carriages, and led many officials in the worship of Buddha by taking up burning incense sticks in their hands. After bowing three times holding the incense, they raised their heads and looked around them. This was indeed a splendid religious hall. You see Dancing flags and banners; Bright, gleaming sunshades. Dancing flags and banners Fill the air with strands of flashing colored mists. Bright, gleaming sunshades Glow in the sun as fiery bolts. Imposing, the gold image of Lokājyeṣṭha;14 Most awesome, the jade features of the arhats. Divine flowers fill the vases. Sandalwood incense burn in the urns. The divine flowers filling the vases Adorn the temple with a brilliant forest of brocade. The sandalwood incense burning in the urns Covers the clear sky with waves of fragrant clouds. Piled high on red trays are fruits in season. On colored counters, mounds of cakes and sweets rest. Rows of noble priests chant the holy sūtras To save from their travails those orphaned souls. Taizong and his officials each lifted the incense; they also worshipped the golden body of the Buddha and paid homage to the arhats. Thereafter, the Master of the Law, Chen Xuanzang, the Grand Expositor of the Faith, led the various monks to greet the Tang emperor. After the ceremony, they went back to their seats according to their rank and station. The priest then presented Taizong with the proclamation for the deliverance of the orphaned souls. It read: The supreme virtue is vast and endless, for Buddhism is founded upon Nirvāṇa. The spirit of the pure and the clean circulates freely and flows everywhere in the Three Regions. There are a thousand changes and ten thousand transformations, all regulated by the forces of yin and yang. Boundless and vast indeed are the substance, the function, the true nature, and the permanence of such phenomena. But look at those orphaned souls, how worthy they are of our pity and commiseration! Now by the holy command of Taizong, we have selected and assembled various priests, who will engage in meditation and in the proclamation of the Law. Flinging wide the gates of salvation and setting in motion many vessels of mercy, we would deliver you, the multitudes, from the Sea of Woe and save you from perdition and from the Sixfold Path. You will be led to return to the way of truth and to enjoy the bliss of Heaven. Whether it be by motion, rest, or nonactivity, you will be united with, and become, pure essences. Therefore make use of this noble occasion, for you are invited to the pleasures of the celestial city. Take advantage of our Grand Mass so that you may find release from Hell’s confinement, ascend quickly and freely to ultimate bliss, and travel without restraint in the Region of the West.15 The poem says: An urn of immortal incense. Some scrolls of salvific power. As we proclaim this boundless Law, Receive now Heaven’s endless grace. All your guilt and crime abolished, You lost souls may leave your prison. May our nation be firmly blessed With peace long and all-embracing. Highly pleased by what he read, Taizong said to the monks, “Be firm, all of you, in your devotion, and do not slack in your service to Buddha. After the achievement of merit and after each has received his blessing, we shall reward you handsomely. Be assured that you will not have labored in vain.”
rationales: Indexes 1 and 3 are from the same paraphraser. Both use the phrase 'who wants to be a cardsharp?' and a very similar structure to express disinterest in clubs/Dussots/spits of land/progress, followed by 'that's all without me,' and then 'he continued, again/once more ignoring the question.' The stylistic choices and sentence order are notably alike, which indicates they are produced by the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 2 and 3 share strikingly similar sentence structures and vocabulary choices, particularly the phrase 'my only care was the progressive reduction in volume which the [sea-]water undergoes as it [becomes deeper/sinks].' Both employ the formal phrasing 'my only care' and use nearly identical clauses, suggesting they were generated by the same paraphraser with only minor word substitutions ('sea-water' vs. 'water', 'as it sinks' vs. 'as it becomes deeper'). The other outputs differ more significantly in structure and word selection.

Outputs 3 and 4 share notable similarities in their sentence structure and word choices—for example, both use "That's... it's an error!" (with slight differences: 'incorrect' vs 'wrong'), and both describe Nina Alexandrovna casting a [somewhat/ almost] regretful glance/gaze at the Prince. The flow, diction, and quasi-formal style are close matches. Compared to the others, which either retain French or are more structurally/dictionally distinct, 3 and 4 are much closer to each other.

Outputs 1 and 3 share notable stylistic similarities: both use the structure of Alyosha addressing Ivan directly by name, explicitly mentioning the fever, and suggesting Ivan lie down and rest his head on the pillow. Both also end with offering a cold compress (though worded slightly differently). Their use of gentle urging ('please', 'have a seat', 'make yourself comfortable'), and a concern for Ivan’s comfort and well-being, as well as the overall sentence structure and level of detail, are very close in both tone and structure, differing only in minor word choices. By contrast, the other outputs either shift the speaker (output 2 is omniscient/third person narrative), use more literal translation or a less smooth flow (outputs 0 and 4 are much closer to the likely original translation).

Outputs 0 and 3 are the closest in terms of stylistic fingerprint. Both use a matter-of-fact tone and similar sentence structures: short declarative sentences, avoidance of quoted speech (compared to 1, 2, and 4, which all use quotation marks for a reported phrase), and a clinical description of withholding details. Both use phrases like 'He recounted the events' (0, 3), 'didn't express emotions' / 'objectively, without dwelling on his personal affections,' and emphasize the lack of elaboration or pressing from the interrogators in mirrored ways. The organization of their information, as well as their overall neutral word choices, suggest they were generated by the same paraphraser.

Outputs 0 and 2 share a very similar narrative structure and vocabulary, both presenting Ivan's realization as a thought process in the third person. They both use the phrase 'Ivan's words trailed off as [he] made a startling/surprising realization' and elaborate in a non-dialogue format, focusing on the internal realization. The choice of 'he hadn't shared that particular information' and 'that specific piece of information didn't come from him' is also strikingly parallel, as is the phrasing about the thought never having crossed his mind, leaving him perplexed. These similarities suggest the same paraphraser was used.

Indexes 2 and 3 both start with a direct description of Mikolka either speaking or standing, they both feature the repeated phrase 'his crowbar still in his hands'/'with the crowbar still in his hands', and both mention his eyes being bloodshot in nearly identical wording. The sentence structures are also quite similar in the way they sequence the actions and descriptions. This consistency in phrasing and structure suggests they were produced by the same paraphraser.

Indexes 0 and 3 share highly similar wording and structure. Both begin with 'As for/to the man', use 'did not even avail/profit himself by the white/clean sheets', and mention 'he blew/snuffed out the candle with his nostril, after the manner of convicts.' The sentence ordering, phraseology, and even choice of idioms are so close that it appears these came from the same paraphrasing system, perhaps with only minimal tweaks.

Outputs 1 and 4 display very similar structure and phrasing. Both start with a sentence about the lack of clarity surrounding Joseph Buquet's death, use phrases like 'the official ruling from the inquest was...' and provide nearly identical wording regarding the event recounted in Moncharmin's memoirs. Both use the phrase 'describes the event this way:' and refer to a 'farewell gathering hosted by Messrs. Debienne and Poligny.' The word choices, sequence, and even minor stylistic elements match far more closely than with the other outputs.

Outputs 0 and 2 both exhibit a very similar approach to paraphrasing the source: they preserve the full narrative arc, focus heavily on detailed, sequential description, and use formal and polished academic language. Both mention highly specific imagery such as 'jade rings and phoenix-feather fans,' 'incense urn,' 'vases overflowed with divine flowers,' and stress both the external spectacle and internal spiritual purpose. Each ends with Taizong's address, and both have structured, indirect quotation with similar tone. Their handling of the proclamation and embedded poem is also nearly identical in content, overall ordering, and phrase choice (e.g., 'urn of immortal incense,' 'scrolls of salvific power,' 'boundless Law'). By contrast, outputs 1, 3, and 4 deviate drastically in form, focus, or content. Output 1 is mostly a poetic list and highly repetitive, output 3 is unrelated and seems from a different narrative, and output 4 takes a more creative, fragmentary approach and diverges from source details. Thus, 0 and 2 clearly stem from the same paraphraser.

human_par3: "Brother, sit down," said Alyosha in alarm. " For goodness' sake, sit down on the sofa! You are delirious; put your head on the pillow, that's right. Would you like a wet towel on your head? Perhaps it will do you good."

He watched anxiously as Ivan did so, then encouraged him to lie back and rest his head on the pillow. Next, Alyosha asked if Ivan would like a cold, damp towel placed on his head, suggesting it might bring some relief."

"Aivan, please have a seat over there on the sofa," Alyosha encouraged, looking worried. "You're burning up with fever. Make yourself comfortable and lie down, rest your head on the pillow. There, is that better? Maybe I can get you a cold compress to put on your head, that might bring some relief."

He told the story, but we shall not go over it again. He told it cursorily, without emotion. Of the ecstasies of his love he did not speak at all. He did, however, tell how his resolve to shoot himself had passed ‘in view of the new facts’. He gave his account without offering reasons or going into detail. human_par3: There was no thunder and no lightning after this. This made the Wizard still more anxious, and he burnt more incense and charms and recited more incantations and struck the table as his fourth signal. Instantly the dragons from the four oceans appeared. Sun cried out above their heads, “Aoguang, where do you think you are going?” Aoguang and his companions came forward and bowed. Then Sun told them what he had told the other helpers before, and added, “You helped me before though not successfully. Today I hope you will help me with success.” The Dragon King replied, “I gladly obey your orders.” Sun said, “I beg of you to give me a helping hand. The Wizard’s signal of the fourth performance is now finished. It is now my turn, and I expect you all to help me. I burn no incense and have no signal but my club. When I lift up my spear the first time, I want the wind to blow.” The Mistress of the Wind said, “The wind shall blow.” “When I lift up my spear the second time, I want the clouds to gather.” The Master of the Clouds said, “The clouds shall gather.” “When I lift up my spear the third time, I want thunder and lightning.” The Master of Thunder and Mistress of Lightning said, “You shall have them in abundance.” “When I lift up my spear the fourth time, I want rain.” The Dragon replied, “Your orders shall be obeyed.” “When I lift up my spear the fifth time, I want a clear sky without fail.” Having arranged this, he restored the false Sun and took his place. He shouted out, “Master Wizard, your fourth signal for performance is now over and there has been neither wind, clouds, thunder, lightning, nor rain. Now it is my time to try.” The Wizard came down from his platform altar and went to the gallery by the king, and the Monkey followed him. The king asked the wizard how it was that none of his four attempts were successful. The wizard replied, “The rain king was not at home today.” The Monkey angrily replied, “The dragon gods were all at home, but your Royal Wizard is not sufficiently skilful.” The king then ordered the Buddhist monks to mount the platform. Sun invited the Master to go, but he said he could not command rain.
llama_31_8b:
A procession of grandeur unfolds with the emperor, accompanied by a thousand guards, marching in front and rear, their flags and adornments glimmering in the light. Halberds, axes, and bludgeons held by dynamic pairs add to the regal atmosphere, while red silk lanterns and a royal incense urn move in perfect harmony. The skies come alive with dragons, phoenixes, falcons, and eagles, as the emperor, a true paragon of virtue, leads the procession, flanked by righteous ministers who have earned the respect of the people. Their wise counsel and Kro faith will bring prosperity to our dynasty, surpassing the great sage, Yu, and the legendary heroes, Shun and Yao, who established endless peace and serenity. The imperial procession weaves through intricate tapestries and sacred archways, glistening with mandarin ducks, umbrellas, robes with dragon designs, jade accessories, and precious sashes, before coming to a majestic gate. The master of the universe, Taizong, steps out of the carriage, assisting the emperor in burning incense, and worshipping the Buddha. The Buddha temple is shining bright, aglow with colorful lanterns and imperial sunshades, which flow like ribbons in the wind.

Thousands of Tang soldiers form a bright march with vital flags, as dignified standards advance far and wide. Master Tianzong, accompanied by other Buddhist monks, comes forward to welcome the emperor. They recite sutras, showering pure voices across the assembly. gemini_25_flash:
A grand procession, featuring numerous lords adorned with jade, guards carrying various weapons and flags, and the stately movement of royal lanterns and incense urns, advanced majestically. Mythical creatures and powerful birds symbolized the righteousness of the emperor, perceived as a wise sage, and his virtuous ministers. Their reign promised centuries of prosperity, surpassing legendary rulers like Yu, Shun, Yao, and Tang, and securing lasting peace. The array was further embellished by ornate umbrellas and robes depicting dragons, creating a dazzling display. Jade rings and phoenix fans swayed through a sacred mist, while caps of pearls, jade belts, purple sashes, and gold medals adorned the attendees. A vast military escort, comprising thousands of soldiers and two lines of marshals, protected the imperial carriage.

The emperor, pure of heart, reverently approached the Buddha, eager to offer incense and seek spiritual merit. The Tang emperor's magnificent retinue soon reached the temple entrance. He halted the music, dismounted, and, joined by many officials, held burning incense sticks to worship the Buddha. After bowing thrice with the incense, they looked around at the awe-inspiring temple.

The temple buzzed with vibrant activity: dancing flags and banners created shimmering waves of color, while gleaming sunshades glowed like fiery bolts. Inside, an impressive golden statue of Lokājyeṣṭha and magnificent jade figures of arhats commanded reverence. Vases brimmed with divine flowers, forming a brilliant brocade-like display, and sandalwood incense burned in urns, its fragrant smoke ascending to veil the clear sky. Red trays were piled high with seasonal fruits, and colorful counters displayed mounds of cakes and sweets. Rows of noble priests chanted sacred sūtras, aiming to deliver orphaned souls from suffering.

Taizong and his officials, after offering incense, also paid homage to the golden Buddha and the arhats. Following this, Chen Xuanzang, the Grand Expositor of the Faith, led the monks in greeting the emperor. After the ceremony, everyone returned to their designated places. The priest then presented Taizong with a proclamation for the deliverance of orphaned souls.

The proclamation began by extolling the boundless virtue of Buddhism, rooted in Nirvāṇa, and the universal flow of purity throughout the Three Regions. It spoke of myriad transformations governed by yin and yang, and emphasized the profound nature and permanence of existence. The document expressed deep pity for orphaned souls and explained that, by Taizong's command, priests had been gathered to meditate and preach the Law. Their mission was to open the gates of salvation and provide compassionate refuge, saving multitudes from suffering and perdition, guiding them back to truth, and leading them to heavenly bliss. It proclaimed that through motion, rest, or nonactivity, these souls would achieve pure essence and were invited to partake in the joys of the celestial city. The Grand Mass offered an opportunity for release from Hell, swift ascent to ultimate bliss, and unrestricted travel to the Western Region.

A poem within the proclamation spoke of an "urn of immortal incense" and "scrolls of salvific power," promising boundless divine grace through the boundless Law. It stated that all guilt and crime would be abolished, allowing lost souls to leave their prison, and prayed for the nation to be firmly blessed with enduring peace.

Taizong, pleased by the message, addressed the monks, urging them to remain steadfast in their devotion and diligent in their service to Buddha. He promised significant rewards for their merits and blessings, assuring them that their efforts would not be in vain.

A magnificent procession, featuring countless lords adorned with jade, guards carrying various weapons, and royal symbols like gilt bludgeons, halberds, axes, red silk lanterns, and the royal incense urn, moved with great solemnity. Mythical creatures like dragons and phoenixes, along with falcons and eagles, were depicted, symbolizing the emperor's power and virtue. This emperor, a righteous sage, and his just ministers were credited with bringing immense happiness and lasting peace to the empire, surpassing even legendary rulers.

The parade included an ornate, curved umbrella and robes embroidered with dragons, their glistening details reflecting off each other. Jade rings and phoenix-feather fans waved amidst a spiritual mist, complementing the pearl caps, jade belts, purple sashes, and gold medals worn by the participants. A vast formation of soldiers, a thousand rows deep, protected the emperor's throne, while two lines of marshals flanked his carriage. The emperor, in a state of spiritual purity, humbly bowed to the Buddha, eager to offer incense and seek good fortune.

The grand imperial procession of the Tang emperor soon arrived at the temple. The emperor commanded the music to cease, dismounted his carriage, and, accompanied by many officials, participated in the worship of Buddha by lighting incense sticks. After bowing respectfully three times with the incense, they looked up to admire the splendid temple hall.

The temple was adorned with vibrant, dancing flags and banners, creating shimmering, colorful mists in the air. Bright, gleaming sunshades glowed like fiery bolts in the sunlight. Inside, a towering golden statue of Lokājyeṣṭha stood imposingly, alongside the awe-inspiring jade figures of the arhats. Vases overflowed with exquisite divine flowers, and sandalwood incense burned in urns. These divine flowers transformed the temple into a brilliant, brocade-like garden, while the burning sandalwood incense filled the clear sky with fragrant clouds. Red trays were piled high with seasonal fruits, and colorful counters displayed mounds of cakes and sweets. Noble priests chanted sacred sūtras in rows, aiming to bring solace to orphaned souls.

Taizong and his officials, after offering incense, also paid homage to the golden Buddha and the arhats. Following this, the Master of the Law, Chen Xuanzang, who was also the Grand Expositor of the Faith, led the monks in greeting the Tang emperor. Once the ceremony concluded, everyone returned to their designated seats according to their rank. The priest then presented Taizong with a proclamation for the spiritual deliverance of orphaned souls.

The proclamation read: Buddhism, founded on Nirvāṇa, possesses boundless and infinite virtue. The pure and clean spirit of the faith pervades and influences all aspects of existence across the Three Regions. Its transformative power is immense, governed by the forces of yin and yang, revealing its boundless nature, function, true essence, and eternal presence. Yet, orphaned souls deserve our profound pity and compassion. By Emperor Taizong's sacred decree, we have gathered various priests to meditate and disseminate the Law. By opening widely the gates of salvation and deploying numerous vessels of mercy, we aim to rescue you, the masses, from the ocean of suffering and save you from damnation and the cycle of rebirth. You will be guided back to the path of truth to experience heavenly bliss. Through activity, rest, or non-activity, you will merge with and become pure essences. Therefore, seize this noble opportunity, as you are invited to partake in the delights of the celestial city. Benefit from our Grand Mass to escape the confines of Hell, ascend swiftly and freely to ultimate happiness, and journey unhindered to the Western Region.

A poem accompanied the proclamation: An urn of immortal incense, some scrolls of salvific power. As we proclaim this boundless Law, receive now Heaven's endless grace. All your guilt and crime abolished, you lost souls may leave your prison. May our nation be firmly blessed with peace long and all-embracing.

Highly pleased with the proclamation, Taizong addressed the monks, saying, "Maintain your strong devotion and diligence in serving the Buddha. After earning merit and receiving your blessings, I will reward you generously. Rest assured, your efforts will not be in vain."

Mythical creatures like dragons and phoenixes, along with falcons and eagles, were depicted, symbolizing the emperor's power and virtue. This emperor, a righteous sage, and his just ministers were credited with bringing immense happiness and lasting peace to the empire, surpassing even legendary rulers.

The parade included an ornate, curved umbrella and robes embroidered with dragons, their glistening details reflecting off each other. Jade rings and phoenix-feather fans waved amidst a spiritual mist, complementing the pearl caps, jade belts, purple sashes, and gold medals worn by the participants. A vast formation of soldiers, a thousand rows deep, protected the emperor's throne, while two lines of marshals flanked his carriage. The emperor, in a state of spiritual purity, humbly bowed to the Buddha, eager to offer incense and seek good fortune.

The grand imperial procession of the Tang emperor soon arrived at the temple. The emperor commanded the music to cease, dismounted his carriage, and, accompanied by many officials, participated in the worship of Buddha by lighting incense sticks. After bowing respectfully three times with the incense, they looked up to admire the splendid temple hall.

The temple was adorned with vibrant, dancing flags and banners, creating shimmering, colorful mists in the air. Bright, gleaming sunshades glowed like fiery bolts in the sunlight.
